title: Fleet Telemetry Snapshot, Q2 2026
text: As of the second quarter of 2026, 1,860 vehicles across Larkspur Autonomy's test and limited commercial fleets run Palisade FE-3.4, the current production release of the fusion engine. Fleet telemetry collected through May 2026 shows a cumulative logged distance of 41.2 million kilometers since the FE-3 program's 2022 launch. The fused perception stack sustains a 20 Hz track-output rate across 96.4 percent of fleet operating hours, with the remainder attributed to planned recalibration windows or degraded-sensor fallback. Mean end-to-end fusion latency, measured from sensor timestamp to fused track output, was 38 milliseconds fleet-wide, with a 99th-percentile latency of 61 milliseconds. Vehicles operating in the Sun Belt test corridor logged the lowest average latency, 34 milliseconds, attributed to more consistent camera exposure conditions reducing preprocessing time relative to the Pacific Northwest and Upper Midwest corridors.

title: How Palisade FE-3 Fuses Camera, Radar, and LIDAR
text: Palisade FE-3 combines seven cameras, five radar units, and three LIDAR sensors into a single fused perception output rather than asking Larkspur Autonomy's planning stack to reconcile separate object lists from each modality. The engine performs track-level fusion: each sensor's raw detections are associated to existing tracks and folded into a shared state estimate maintained by an unscented Kalman filter, rather than fusing raw point clouds and pixels directly. This choice trades some theoretical accuracy against full low-level fusion for a substantially simpler pipeline that tolerates a sensor dropping out without collapsing the whole estimate. Camera detections anchor object classification and bearing, radar contributes direct range-rate measurement that is largely unaffected by rain or fog, and LIDAR supplies precise three-dimensional geometry the other two modalities cannot match on their own. A central track manager owns the lifecycle of every object the vehicle is tracking, deciding when a new detection should spawn a track, when two tracks likely describe the same object, and when a track should be dropped.

title: Sensor Suite Hardware Interface Specifications
text: Palisade FE-3's standard sensor suite ships with seven 8-megapixel HDR global-shutter cameras running at 30 frames per second, covering a combined 360-degree field of view with overlapping corner coverage. The forward long-range radar operates at 77 GHz FMCW, reaching 260 meters with an 18-degree field of view, while four corner radars each cover 110 meters across a 150-degree field of view at a 20 Hz update rate. The roof-mounted mechanical LIDAR unit spins at 10 Hz, resolves 128 channels, and reaches a maximum range of 200 meters at 10 percent target reflectivity, producing roughly 2.4 million points per second. Two solid-state corner LIDAR units extend coverage to 80 meters at a 15 Hz refresh rate in the vehicle's blind-spot zones. All eight LIDAR and radar units and all seven cameras connect to the Ironwood module over a shared interconnect rated at 3.8 gigabytes per second of aggregate bandwidth.

title: Sensor Suite Evolution Across Palisade Generations
text:
Larkspur Autonomy's three Palisade generations differ substantially in sensor suite, maximum detection range, and the tracking filter used to fuse them, summarized below.

| Generation | Sensor suite | Max detection range | Tracking filter |
|---|---|---|---|
| FE-1 (2016) | Camera only | 80 m | Constant-velocity Kalman filter |
| FE-2 (2019) | Camera + radar | 200 m | Extended Kalman filter |
| FE-3 (2022-present) | Camera + radar + LIDAR | 260 m | Unscented Kalman filter |

Each generation's filter change followed directly from a sensor addition that introduced motion or measurement nonlinearity the previous filter could no longer handle cleanly.

title: Sensor Calibration Procedure
text:
Before a Palisade FE-3 vehicle is released from a service bay back into fleet operation, technicians run the following calibration sequence in full. 1. Verify intrinsic camera calibration against a checkerboard target at a fixed 4-meter distance, rejecting any camera whose reprojection error exceeds 0.3 pixels.
2. Confirm LIDAR-to-camera extrinsic alignment by reprojecting a static point cloud into each camera frame and checking that reprojection error stays below 2 centimeters at 20 meters.
3. Validate each radar unit's boresight alignment against a corner-reflector target placed at a known bearing and range.
4. Trigger a PTP handshake across all sensor NICs and confirm the Ironwood module reports cross-sensor skew below 50 microseconds before enabling fusion.
5. Run a five-minute static validation scene and confirm the fused track output matches surveyed ground-truth positions within 5 centimeters. A vehicle failing any single step is held in the bay rather than returned to service on a partial pass.

title: Track Association Gating Threshold
text: Palisade FE-3 decides whether a new sensor detection belongs to an existing track using a gating test on the normalized innovation, rather than a fixed-radius distance cutoff. For a detection z with predicted measurement Hx and innovation covariance S, the engine computes the squared Mahalanobis distance d^2 = (z - Hx)^T * S^-1 * (z - Hx) and accepts the association only if d^2 falls below a chi-squared threshold. With a two-dimensional measurement, bearing and range, the engine uses the chi-squared value at the 99 percent confidence level for two degrees of freedom, 9.21, rather than the more permissive 95 percent value some earlier prototypes used. A fixed-radius gate was rejected during FE-2 development because it accepted or rejected detections identically regardless of a track's own uncertainty, causing false associations for tracks with poorly constrained velocity estimates and unnecessary rejections for tracks the filter had already converged on tightly.

title: Citing Radar-Vision Fusion Research
text: Palisade FE-3's association logic draws on a body of tracking research published well beyond the English-language venues Larkspur Autonomy's engineers cite most often internally. Zhang and Liu's 2018 paper, 面向自动驾驶车辆的雷达-视觉融合目标跟踪方法研究 (Research on radar-vision fusion object tracking methods for autonomous vehicles), examines exactly the mismatch between radar's sparse, high-confidence range-rate returns and camera's dense but range-ambiguous bounding boxes that motivated FE-2's original gating design. The paper's central argument, that association should weight each modality's contribution by its own measurement covariance rather than treating radar and camera detections symmetrically, matches the covariance-weighted approach FE-3's track manager still uses today. Larkspur Autonomy's internal tracking specification cites the paper directly in its bibliography, alongside the chi-squared gating literature referenced in the association threshold section.

title: Time-Synchronization Subsystem
text: Palisade FE-3 synchronizes all sensor timestamps using hardware-timestamped Precision Time Protocol under IEEE 1588-2019, with the grandmaster clock hosted on the Ironwood compute module and a boundary clock at every camera, radar, and LIDAR network interface. The subsystem is specified to hold steady-state cross-sensor skew within a plus-or-minus 50 microsecond budget under normal operation. Fleet telemetry collected during May 2026 measured a 95th-percentile cross-sensor skew of 22 microseconds, comfortably inside that budget across 1,860 monitored vehicles. Since firmware version FE-3.2, released in September 2024, the fusion engine automatically falls back to a camera-and-radar-only tracking mode if measured skew exceeds 5 milliseconds continuously for more than 200 milliseconds, re-enabling LIDAR fusion only once skew has stayed below the 50 microsecond budget for a full 10-second window.

title: Track States and Occlusion Handling
text: A Palisade FE-3 track moves through four states over its lifetime: tentative, confirmed, coasting, and lost. A tentative track exists after a single detection and is promoted to confirmed only once three consecutive cycles produce a matching association, filtering out most spurious single-frame detections before they ever reach the planning stack. A confirmed track that stops receiving matching detections, typically because another vehicle or roadside object has occluded it, enters the coasting state, where the unscented Kalman filter continues propagating the track's position and velocity forward using its motion model alone, without a measurement update. Coasting is capped at 2.5 seconds, fifty cycles at the engine's 20 Hz output rate, after which an unconfirmed track is dropped to lost rather than coasted indefinitely on a stale prediction. When a detection reappears near a coasting track's predicted position, the track manager re-associates it using the same Mahalanobis gate applied to fresh detections, plus a secondary check against a learned appearance embedding, requiring cosine similarity of at least 0.82 before restoring confirmed status.

title: Spec Addendum: LIDAR Range Clarification
text: This addendum clarifies a point raised by two integration partners regarding roof LIDAR range figures published in the hardware interface specification. The roof-mounted mechanical LIDAR unit spins at 10 Hz, resolves 128 channels, and reaches a maximum range of 230 meters at 10 percent target reflectivity, producing roughly 2.4 million points per second. This figure is the unit's rated range under the reflectivity condition specified in its datasheet; range degrades meaningfully against low-reflectivity targets such as dark, non-retroreflective clothing, which is why the perception stack applies a separate, more conservative detection-range assumption during planning. Integration partners building custom sensor mounts should reference this addendum rather than earlier partner-facing documentation, which predates the FE-3.3 release and used a slightly different measurement methodology.

title: Track Retention by Occlusion Duration
text:
FE-3.4 field data from the 2026 fleet shows how track retention and re-association success both degrade as occlusion duration approaches the 2.5-second coasting cap.

| Occlusion duration | Track retention rate | Re-association success rate |
|---|---|---|
| 0.0-0.5 s | 99.2% | 98.6% |
| 0.5-1.5 s | 94.7% | 91.3% |
| 1.5-2.5 s | 81.5% | 74.9% |
| >2.5 s | 0.0% | 41.2% |

The zero percent retention rate beyond 2.5 seconds is by design, since tracks coasting that long are dropped to lost regardless of whether a matching detection would have reappeared moments later; the nonzero re-association figure in that row reflects lost tracks the track manager re-initiates as new confirmed tracks quickly enough that downstream planning treats the gap as negligible.

title: Citing Finnish Time-Synchronization Research
text: The reliability targets behind FE-3's PTP-based time-synchronization subsystem draw partly on Nordic robotics research that predates Larkspur Autonomy's own program. Korhonen and Virtanen's 2015 study, Monianturifuusion aikasynkronoinnin tarkkuus itseohjautuvissa ajoneuvoissa (The accuracy of multi-sensor fusion time synchronization in self-driving vehicles), conducted at the Pohjolan teknillinen tutkimuskeskus, measured how synchronization error propagates into track-position error under a range of sensor combinations similar to FE-3's own camera-radar-LIDAR suite. The study's finding, that timestamp skew below roughly 100 microseconds contributes negligibly to fused position error compared to sensor measurement noise itself, informed the 50 microsecond budget FE-3's synchronization subsystem specification later adopted with a comfortable margin. Larkspur Autonomy's synchronization architecture document cites the study directly alongside later IEEE 1588 profile work.

title: Unscented Kalman Filter Tuning Parameters
text: FE-3's track filter uses standard unscented Kalman filter sigma-point parameters: alpha = 0.001, kappa = 0, and beta = 2, tuned during FE-3's 2021-2022 development cycle and unchanged since the FE-3.0 release. Process noise is modeled per motion component, with a longitudinal acceleration standard deviation of 1.2 meters per second squared and a lateral standard deviation of 0.5, reflecting that tracked objects accelerate more sharply along their heading than across it. Measurement noise is tuned per sensor: camera bearing carries a 0.3-degree standard deviation, radar range and range-rate carry 0.15-meter and 0.1-meter-per-second deviations respectively, and LIDAR position carries a 0.05-meter standard deviation. The filter outputs a fused track update every 50 milliseconds, a 20 Hz cycle rate, and buffers out-of-sequence measurements for up to five cycles, 250 milliseconds, to accommodate the LIDAR's slower 10 Hz native sampling rate relative to the fusion cycle.

title: Time-Sync Fault Recovery Checklist
text:
When onboard diagnostics flag cross-sensor timestamp skew outside the normal budget, the fusion engine and remote monitoring team follow this recovery sequence. 1. Ironwood diagnostics monitor cross-sensor skew and log a warning once skew exceeds 1 millisecond, above the 50 microsecond steady-state budget but short of functional impact.
2. If skew exceeds 5 milliseconds continuously for more than 200 milliseconds, the engine switches to camera-and-radar-only tracking and suppresses LIDAR track updates.
3. The Ironwood module attempts a boundary-clock resync against the grandmaster, and if that fails, forces a grandmaster re-election among the remaining synchronized sensor NICs.
4. Remote monitoring confirms skew has returned below the 50 microsecond budget and held there for a full 10-second window before flagging the vehicle eligible for re-enablement.
5. The vehicle re-enables LIDAR fusion automatically and logs the incident, including skew magnitude and duration, for later review. Most flagged incidents resolve at step three without a service-bay visit.

title: History: From Camera-Only Perception to Full Sensor Fusion
text:
Palisade began in 2016 as a camera-only perception system, FE-1, built around seven cameras and a nearest-neighbor tracker running a constant-velocity Kalman filter. That approach worked reasonably well on clear days but degraded sharply in rain, fog, and low light, and it struggled to estimate an object's velocity accurately from a single frame's bearing measurement alone, since depth had to be inferred rather than measured directly. Early FE-1 deployments in the Pacific Northwest test corridor recorded velocity-estimation errors nearly three times larger during rain than in clear weather, a gap that camera-only processing improvements could narrow but never close.

The team's first serious response was FE-2, introduced in 2019, which added five radar units specifically for their direct, weather-robust range-rate measurement. Radar's velocity readings do not depend on visual contrast the way camera-derived optical flow does, so fusing radar range-rate into the track state meaningfully closed the wet-weather velocity gap. Radar's nonlinear relationship between Cartesian track state and polar range-rate measurement, however, exceeded what the constant-velocity Kalman filter's linear measurement model could represent, motivating the switch to an extended Kalman filter, which linearizes that relationship anew at each update using the measurement's Jacobian.

FE-3, launched in 2022, added LIDAR for precise three-dimensional geometry and sharper occlusion-boundary detection than camera or radar alone could provide. LIDAR's addition, combined with FE-2 field data showing the EKF's linearization error grew noticeably during sharp vehicle maneuvers, prompted a harder look at filter choice. The team evaluated three candidates: continuing with the EKF, adopting a particle filter, and adopting an unscented Kalman filter. The particle filter offered the best accuracy under strong nonlinearity but at a computational cost the Ironwood module's power budget could not sustain at fleet scale. The unscented Kalman filter, which propagates a small deterministic set of sigma points through the true nonlinear motion and measurement models rather than linearizing them, matched the particle filter's accuracy on FE-3's maneuvering-target test suite closely enough, at a fraction of the computational cost, to become FE-3's production filter.

The synchronization architecture evolved in step. FE-1 and FE-2 relied on software NTP timestamping, adequate when only cameras and radar needed rough alignment, but LIDAR's spinning geometry made sub-millisecond alignment error visible as measurable point-cloud smear at highway speed. FE-3 replaced NTP with hardware PTP timestamping specifically to close that gap, a change the engineering team now considers as consequential to FE-3's accuracy gains as the LIDAR sensor addition itself, and one that later made the tighter chi-squared gating thresholds described in the association section possible in the first place, since those thresholds assume timing precision NTP could never have reliably delivered.

title: Incident Postmortem: The March 2024 Time-Sync Drift Event
text:
On March 14, 2024, a Palisade FE-3 test vehicle operating on Larkspur Autonomy's closed validation course near Flagstaff, Arizona, experienced a sustained sensor time-synchronization fault during a scheduled pedestrian-occlusion test scenario. The vehicle was running firmware FE-3.1 at the time, several months before the FE-3.2 release that later added automatic fallback behavior for exactly this failure mode.

The fault began when the Ironwood module's primary PTP grandmaster clock failed a routine health check and the sensor network failed over to its designated backup grandmaster. Post-incident analysis traced the underlying cause to a firmware bug in the boundary-clock switching logic on the roof LIDAR's network interface: during failover, the boundary clock briefly applied a stale offset correction left over from the previous grandmaster's clock domain, rather than recomputing the offset fresh against the new grandmaster. The result was a sustained 38-millisecond timestamp offset between the LIDAR and the camera-and-radar sensor group, persisting for approximately four minutes before an unrelated software restart cleared the stale offset.

During that four-minute window, the fusion engine's track association gate, tuned assuming timestamp skew well under the 50-microsecond design budget, began systematically misassociating LIDAR returns against predicted track positions computed from stale camera-and-radar-derived timestamps. The tracker spawned two duplicate ghost tracks for a single static test obstacle. More seriously, a pedestrian mannequin used in the occlusion test scenario, correctly tracked while visible, entered a scripted occlusion behind a parked vehicle; when the mannequin reappeared, the 38-millisecond offset pushed the LIDAR-derived reappearance position outside the coasting track's Mahalanobis gate, and the track manager failed to re-associate it. The mannequin was briefly re-classified as a new tentative track rather than restored to its existing confirmed track, a three-cycle gap, 150 milliseconds, during which the confirmed-track safety interlock the planning stack relies on was not asserted.

No vehicle-mannequin contact occurred; the test scenario's safety driver had independent visual confirmation and manual braking authority throughout. The investigation's primary finding was that the boundary-clock switching bug, not the association gate itself, was the true root cause, and the team's main remediation was a boundary-clock firmware fix requiring a fresh offset computation on every grandmaster failover rather than reusing a cached value. A secondary remediation, shipped in FE-3.2, added the automatic camera-and-radar-only fallback mode described in the synchronization subsystem specification, so that any future skew fault of comparable magnitude degrades gracefully rather than silently corrupting association decisions. The fix was validated against a reproduction of the exact failover sequence before being cleared for fleet-wide deployment in September 2024.

title: FAQ: Fusion Cycle Rate and Filter Update Timing
text:
Q: How often does Palisade FE-3 publish a new fused track update to the planning stack?
A: The filter outputs a fused track update every 40 milliseconds, a 20 Hz cycle rate, and buffers out-of-sequence measurements for up to five cycles, 250 milliseconds, to accommodate the LIDAR's slower 10 Hz native sampling rate relative to the fusion cycle. This is the rate most planning-stack partners have tuned their downstream controllers against. Partners occasionally ask whether this rate is configurable per vehicle; it is not exposed as a runtime setting, since changing it would require re-tuning the unscented Kalman filter's process-noise parameters to match a different prediction interval. A smaller number of partners ask about output jitter rather than the nominal rate; measured jitter around the nominal cycle stays under 2 milliseconds fleet-wide. Questions about the out-of-sequence measurement buffer, which separately accommodates LIDAR's slower sampling rate, are addressed in the filter tuning parameters section.

title: Ironwood Compute Platform and the Fusion Latency Budget
text: The Ironwood compute module hosts every stage of the Palisade FE-3 pipeline, from raw sensor capture through fused track output, across a dual-SoC design rated at 320 TOPS combined. Each fusion cycle moves through five stages: sensor capture and hardware timestamping, per-modality preprocessing, cross-modality association, unscented Kalman filter update, and track-state management before handoff to the planning stack. Larkspur Autonomy budgets latency per stage rather than only end to end, since a single slow stage can silently eat the margin every other stage depends on. Sensor capture and timestamping is budgeted at 4 milliseconds, preprocessing at 14 milliseconds, association at 6 milliseconds, the filter update itself at 9 milliseconds, and track-state management at 5 milliseconds, summing to a 38-millisecond target that leaves headroom under the engine's 45-millisecond hard latency ceiling. Preprocessing consumes the largest share of the budget because LIDAR point-cloud filtering and camera object-detection inference both run there; profiling during FE-3.3 development found LIDAR filtering alone responsible for roughly half of preprocessing's total cost.

title: 2025 Winter Test Campaign Results
text: Larkspur Autonomy ran its 2025 winter test campaign at a closed course near Houghton, Michigan, from January 6 through February 14, 2025, logging 2,140 vehicle-hours across sustained snowfall and sub-freezing conditions. Snow depth during active testing ranged from 5 to 46 centimeters, with roughly 60 percent of test hours conducted in depths exceeding 15 centimeters. Camera-based detection recall dropped by 18 percent relative to the dry-pavement baseline under heavy snowfall, while radar-based detection recall dropped by only 4 percent over the same conditions. LIDAR point-cloud density fell by an average of 31 percent in falling snow due to scattering returns the perception stack must filter as noise. Fused detection recall across all three modalities together held at 96.1 percent of the dry-pavement baseline, a 2.3 percentage point improvement over the equivalent 2024 winter test campaign result.
